## MCP Server Changes = DUAL-REPO PUSH (MANDATORY)

### MCP Server Changes = DUAL-REPO PUSH (MANDATORY)

**This monorepo deploys to TWO separate git repositories:**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  When you modify files in apps/mcp-server/src/                              │
│                                                                             │
│  YOU MUST PUSH TO BOTH REPOS:                                               │
│                                                                             │
│  1. RLMSaas (this repo)     → github.com/alopez3006/snipara                 │
│     └── Push here first     → Deploys to: Vercel + PyPI                     │
│                                                                             │
│  2. snipara-fastapi (separate) → github.com/alopez3006/snipara-fastapi      │
│     └── Copy files & push      → Deploys to: api.snipara.com (Railway)      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**After committing to RLMSaas, run:**

```bash
# Copy changed files
cp apps/mcp-server/src/<file>.py /Users/alopez/Devs/snipara-fastapi/src/

# Push to snipara-fastapi
cd /Users/alopez/Devs/snipara-fastapi
git add src/<file>.py
git commit -m "<same message>"
git push origin main
```

**Files requiring dual-repo sync:** `server.py`, `db.py`, `auth.py`, `rlm_engine.py`, `mcp_transport.py`, `usage.py`, `config.py`, `models.py`, `services/*.py`

See [docs/DEPLOYMENT_ARCHITECTURE.md](docs/DEPLOYMENT_ARCHITECTURE.md) for full details.

## Claude Desktop

### Claude Desktop

Add to `~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json` (macOS) or `%APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json` (Windows):

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "snipara": {
      "command": "uvx",
      "args": ["snipara-mcp"],
      "env": {
        "SNIPARA_API_KEY": "sk-your-api-key",
        "SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID": "your-project-id"
      }
    }
  }
}
```


---

## Claude Code (Full Automation)

#### Claude Code (Full Automation)

| Automation | How It Works | User Setting |
|------------|--------------|--------------|
| **Auto-inject context before file reads** | PreToolUse hook calls `rlm_context_query` | Toggle: On/Off |
| **Track accessed files** | PostToolUse hook calls `rlm_inject` | Toggle: On/Off |
| **Preserve context on compaction** | PreCompact hook saves to RLM | Toggle: On/Off |
| **Restore context on session start** | SessionStart hook loads from RLM | Toggle: On/Off |
| **Enrich every prompt** | UserPromptSubmit hook adds docs | Toggle: On/Off |

**Generated `.claude/settings.json`:**

```json
{
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Read|Glob|Grep|Edit",
        "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "rlm-hook pre-tool", "timeout": 10 }]
      }
    ],
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Read|Edit|Write",
        "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "rlm-hook post-tool", "timeout": 5 }]
      }
    ],
    "PreCompact": [
      {
        "matcher": "auto|manual",
        "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "rlm-hook pre-compact", "timeout": 30 }]
      }
    ],
    "SessionStart": [
      {
        "matcher": "compact|resume",
        "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "rlm-hook session-restore", "timeout": 10 }]
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

## 👥 Team Standards (Auto-Enforce Rules)

### 👥 Team Standards (Auto-Enforce Rules)

**Plan Required:** 👥 TEAM ($49/mo) or ENTERPRISE

**Tool:** `rlm_shared_context`
**Use when:** Your team needs consistent coding practices
**Setup once:** Upload coding standards to Shared Collection
**Every dev gets:** Auto-injected team rules in every query

```json
{
  "categories": ["MANDATORY", "BEST_PRACTICES"],
  "max_tokens": 4000
}
```

**Categories by priority:**

- `MANDATORY` - Non-negotiable rules (security, architecture)
- `BEST_PRACTICES` - Recommended patterns (40% token budget)
- `GUIDELINES` - Helpful suggestions
- `REFERENCE` - Background info

⚠️ **Not available on Free/Pro plans** - Team-wide features require Team plan.

## Phase 8: RLM Runtime (Open Source)

### 8.1 RLM Runtime Package

**Status:** [x] Complete (January 2026)

**Goal:** Create an open-source runtime that enables LLMs to recursively decompose tasks, execute code in sandboxed environments, and optionally integrate with Snipara for context optimization.

**Repository:** https://github.com/alopez3006/rlm-runtime

**Package:** `rlm-runtime` on PyPI

**Implementation Notes:**

- Apache 2.0 licensed open-source project
- Standalone execution without Snipara dependency
- Snipara integration as optional plugin
- Full trajectory logging for debugging

**Tasks:**

- [x] Create project scaffold at `/Users/alopez/Devs/rlm-runtime/`
- [x] Implement core orchestrator (`src/rlm/core/orchestrator.py`)
- [x] Implement Local REPL with RestrictedPython sandbox
- [x] Implement Docker REPL for isolated execution
- [x] Create LiteLLM backend for 100+ LLM providers
- [x] Build CLI interface (`rlm run`, `rlm init`, `rlm logs`, `rlm doctor`)
- [x] Add trajectory logging (JSONL format)
- [x] Create builtin tools (execute_code, file_read, list_files)
- [x] Write comprehensive documentation (README.md)
- [x] Add Apache 2.0 license

**Architecture:**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  RLM Orchestrator                                               │
│  • Manages recursion depth and token budgets                    │
│  • Coordinates LLM calls and tool execution                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  LLM Backends              │  REPL Environments                 │
│  • LiteLLM (default)       │  • Local (RestrictedPython)        │
│  • OpenAI                  │  • Docker (isolated)               │
│  • Anthropic               │                                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Tool Registry                                                  │
│  • Builtin: file_read, execute_code                            │
│  • Snipara: context_query, sections, search (optional)         │
│  • Custom: user-defined tools                                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Project Structure:**

```
rlm-runtime/
├── src/rlm/
│   ├── core/
│   │   ├── orchestrator.py      # Main RLM class
│   │   ├── config.py            # Configuration loading
│   │   └── exceptions.py        # Domain errors
│   ├── backends/
│   │   ├── base.py              # Backend/Tool protocols
│   │   └── litellm.py           # LiteLLM adapter
│   ├── repl/
│   │   ├── base.py              # REPL protocol
│   │   ├── local.py             # RestrictedPython sandbox
│   │   └── docker.py            # Docker isolation
│   ├── tools/
│   │   └── builtin/             # Built-in tools
│   ├── logging/
│   │   └── trajectory.py        # JSONL logging
│   └── cli/
│       ├── main.py              # CLI entry point
│       └── commands/            # CLI commands
├── tests/                       # Test suite
├── pyproject.toml               # Package config
├── README.md                    # Documentation
└── LICENSE                      # Apache 2.0
```

## OAuth Device Flow (MCP Authentication)

## OAuth Device Flow (MCP Authentication)

OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628) enables secure MCP authentication without copying API keys. Users run a CLI command, visit a URL, enter a code, and authenticate via browser.

## Sprint 2: Docker Sandbox + CLI Setup

### Sprint 2: Docker Sandbox + CLI Setup

Goals:
- DockerREPL sandbox with isolated filesystem and resource limits.
- CLI to manage local runtime and Docker lifecycle.
- Configurable tool budgets and per-tool timeouts.

Prompts:
- "Add DockerREPL and run REPL code in a container."
- "Add CLI commands to start/stop the local runtime."
- "Enforce tool budgets and per-tool timeouts."

Tests:
- Integration: DockerREPL executes a safe script with no host FS access.
- Regression: local REPL still works.

Security:
- Disable outbound network in DockerREPL by default.
- Read-only mount of working directory (if needed).
- Resource limits: CPU, memory, and wall-clock timeout.


---

## Why a REPL (and Example)

## Why a REPL (and Example)

The REPL executes real code so the model does not have to "simulate" computation
in tokens. This is cheaper, more reliable, and easier to audit.

Example: extract the most frequent endpoint paths from raw logs.

Prompt:

```
Find the top 5 API routes by request count and return a JSON object.
```

RLM plan (simplified):
1. Call Snipara context tools to locate the log file format.
2. Use REPL to parse and count routes.
3. Return a compact JSON summary to the model.

REPL snippet (illustrative):

```python
from collections import Counter

routes = []
with open("logs/api.log", "r") as f:
    for line in f:
        # Example line format: "GET /api/projects 200 12ms"
        parts = line.split()
        if len(parts) >= 2 and parts[1].startswith("/api/"):
            routes.append(parts[1])

top = Counter(routes).most_common(5)
result = {route: count for route, count in top}
```

The LLM receives only the small JSON result, not the full logs or the full
computation trace, which reduces token usage and cost.


## Environments


### Local
- Fastest iteration.
- Runs in-process, limited isolation.
- Suitable for dev or trusted inputs only.


### Docker (Local)
- Stronger isolation on the user's machine.
- Configurable base image.
- Recommended default for safe local execution.

## POWER USER TOOLS

### POWER USER TOOLS

| Tool              | Use When                                          | Example                                  |
| ----------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| `rlm_multi_query` | Multiple related questions in batch               | Query auth + billing + users in parallel |
| `rlm_decompose`   | Want to inspect/modify sub-queries before running | Complex research with manual control     |
| `rlm_plan`        | Want to see execution plan before running         | Audit/debug complex query workflows      |
| `rlm_read`        | Expand context around specific line numbers       | Editor integrations showing line ranges  |


---

## First-Query Tool Tips

## First-Query Tool Tips

On the **first query of each session**, Snipara automatically injects a brief tool guide into your context. This helps users discover available tools without reading documentation.

**How it works:**

1. First `rlm_context_query` call in a session includes tool tips (~300-500 tokens)
2. Subsequent queries in the same session do NOT include tips
3. Tips are **plan-filtered** - users only see tools available to their plan
4. Tips update automatically when plan changes (next session)

**Response field:**

```json
{
  "first_query_tips_included": true, // Only true on first query
  "session_context_included": true
}
```

**Tips by plan:**

| Plan      | Tools Shown                                                                   |
| --------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Free**  | Primary tools (`rlm_context_query`, `rlm_ask`, `rlm_search`) + Utility        |
| **Pro**   | + Power user tools (`rlm_multi_query`, `rlm_decompose`, `rlm_shared_context`) |
| **Team+** | + Team tools (`rlm_multi_project_query`, `rlm_plan`, templates)               |

This feature is automatic and requires no configuration.

[![PyPI version](https://badge.fury.io/py/snipara-mcp.svg)](https://pypi.org/project/snipara-mcp/)
[![Python 3.10+](https://img.shields.io/badge/python-3.10+-blue.svg)](https://www.python.org/downloads/)

MCP server for [Snipara](https://snipara.com) - Context optimization and Agent infrastructure for LLMs.

**Two Products in One:**

- **Snipara** - Context optimization with 90% token reduction
- **Snipara Agents** - Multi-agent memory, swarms, and coordination

**v2.1.0:** Full tool parity with FastAPI server - all 39 tools now available via stdio transport!

Works with any MCP-compatible client including Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, Claude Code, Gemini, GPT, and more.

**LLM-agnostic**: Snipara optimizes context delivery - you use your own LLM (Claude, GPT, Gemini, Llama, etc.).


## Installation


### Option 1: uvx (Recommended - No Install)

```bash
uvx snipara-mcp
```


### Option 2: pip

```bash
pip install snipara-mcp
```

prompt:
{'sections': [{'title': '6. MCP Server Issues', 'content': '### 6. MCP Server Issues
```bash
# Check MCP server status
curl -s https://api.snipara.com/health

# Test MCP endpoint
curl -s -X POST "https://api.snipara.com/mcp/PROJECT_SLUG" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \

', 'file': '.claude/commands/debug.md', 'lines': [54, 64], 'relevance_score': 1.0, 'token_count': 108, 'truncated': False}, {'title': 'Snipara MCP Server', 'content': '# Snipara MCP Server

[![PyPI version](https://badge.fury.io/py/snipara-mcp.svg)](https://pypi.org/project/snipara-mcp/)
[![Python 3.10+](https://img.shields.io/badge/python-3.10+-blue.svg)](https://www.python.org/downloads/)
[![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-yellow.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

MCP server for [Snipara](https://snipara.com) - Context optimization and Agent infrastructure for LLMs.

**Two Products in One:**

- **Snipara** - Context optimization with 90% token reduction
- **Snipara Agents** - Multi-agent memory, swarms, and coordination

**v2.1.0:** Full tool parity with FastAPI server - all 39 tools now available via stdio transport!

Works with any MCP-compatible client including Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, Claude Code, Gemini, GPT, and more.

**LLM-agnostic**: Snipara optimizes context delivery - you use your own LLM (Claude, GPT, Gemini, Llama, etc.).


## Installation


### Option 1: uvx (Recommended - No Install)

```bash
uvx snipara-mcp
```


### Option 2: pip

```bash
pip install snipara-mcp
```

```bash
cd apps/mcp-server/snipara-mcp
# Mettre à jour version dans pyproject.toml et __init__.py
rm -rf dist/
/opt/miniconda3/bin/python -m build
/opt/miniconda3/bin/python -m twine upload dist/*
```

**Version actuelle:** 1.1.0

', 'file': '.claude/commands/debug.md', 'lines': [38617, 38620], 'relevance_score': 0.8847581928461059, 'token_count': 68, 'truncated': False}, {'title': 'Per-Project MCP Configuration', 'content': '### Per-Project MCP Configuration

Each project has its own Snipara MCP server configured via `.mcp.json` in the project root.

**Configuration file: `.mcp.json`**

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "<project-slug>": {
      "type": "http",
      "url": "https://api.snipara.com/mcp/<project-slug>",
      "headers": {
        "X-API-Key": "<your-api-key>"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Current project configurations:**

| Project           | Slug          | Endpoint                          |
| ----------------- | ------------- | --------------------------------- |
| RLMSaas (Snipara) | `snipara`     | `api.snipara.com/mcp/snipara`     |
| Vibe Coding       | `vibe-coding` | `api.snipara.com/mcp/vibe-coding` |
| ZorAI             | `zorai`       | `api.snipara.com/mcp/zorai`       |
| Dubgrr            | `dubgrr`      | `api.snipara.com/mcp/dubgrr`      |
| SwapLoom          | `swaploom`    | `api.snipara.com/mcp/swaploom`    |

1. Edit `apps/mcp-server/src/<file>.py`
2. **MUST duplicate to `snipara-fastapi` repo** (see Architecture section)

4. Push both repos

response:
Below is a end-to-end trace of an MCP request starting in Claude Desktop and ending with a database query + the response back to Claude. I include both the common local-process (snipara-mcp bridge) path shown in your Claude Desktop example and the direct-HTTP path (alternate configuration). I call out the key code/files the request passes through and noteworthy behaviors (auth, tool dispatch, token-budgeting, errors).

High-level summary (one-line)
- Claude Desktop → MCP client/bridge (snipara-mcp) or direct HTTP → Snipara MCP server (FastAPI/stdio handler) → tool dispatch (rlm tools/services) → DB layer (db.py / models.py / services/*.py) → result assembled → JSON-RPC response → client → Claude Desktop.

Detailed step-by-step flow

1) Claude Desktop starts/addresses an MCP server
- In your example config Claude Desktop runs a command:
  - command: "uvx", args: ["snipara-mcp"]
  - env includes SNIPARA_API_KEY and SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID
- That causes Claude Desktop to spawn the snipara-mcp process and speak MCP (JSON-RPC) to it over stdio (local bridge).
- Alternate config: .mcp.json may specify an HTTP endpoint (e.g., https://api.snipara.com/mcp/<project-slug>) in which case Claude Desktop talks directly to the HTTP server instead of launching a local process.

2) Claude Desktop issues a tool request (JSON-RPC / MCP)
- Example: A tool call like rlm_context_query or tools/list sent as JSON-RPC:
  { "jsonrpc":"2.0", "id":1, "method":"tools/list", "params": {...} }
- Over stdio this is sent to the snipara-mcp process. Over HTTP the client encodes the same payload in the HTTP body.

3) snipara-mcp (local bridge / client) behavior
- snipara-mcp is the client-side package that bridges MCP requests to Snipara backend.
- It receives the JSON-RPC call on stdio, adds authentication headers (from SNIPARA_API_KEY or env), maps project ID (SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID), and forwards an HTTP request to the backend API endpoint (e.g., https://api.snipara.com/mcp/<project>).
- If snipara-mcp is used as a pure HTTP client (when installed and configured directly), it similarly signs the request and POSTs to the server endpoint.

4) HTTP request to backend (api.snipara.com/mcp/<project>)
- The bridge POSTs JSON-RPC to the server endpoint and includes auth (X-API-Key or Authorization header).
- You can test this directly with curl (docs include an example curl health / tools/list).

5) Entry on server: transport & auth (server.py / mcp_transport.py)
- The FastAPI/stdio server code (server.py, mcp_transport.py) accepts the request.
- mcp_transport.py validates the API key, project id and the JSON-RPC envelope.
  - If auth fails, mcp_transport.py / server.py return structured invalid-key/error messages (the codebase has explicit messages for missing/invalid keys).
- If valid, the server parses method and params, and hands the request to the MCP dispatcher.

6) Tool dispatch & request routing (tools / rlm_engine / services)
- The server maps JSON-RPC method names to tool handlers (examples: rlm_context_query, search, read, sections, decompose, etc.).
- The rlm-context tools implement token budgeting / semantic search logic:
  - rlm_context_query may perform multi-step retrieval: metadata search, section assembly, summarization, token-budget allocation.
  - First-query behavior: the server may inject tool tips (first_query_tips) on the first session call.
- Some tools call into higher-level orchestrators (rlm_engine / rlm-runtime) when execution or decomposition is needed (e.g., decompose, multi_query, or when a REPL is invoked).

7) Service layer → DB layer (services/*.py, db.py, models.py)
- When the requested tool needs persisted data (docs, sections, project config, user state), it calls the service functions which use the DB layer.
- Typical chain:
  - service function (services/*.py) builds a query,
  - uses models.py (ORM models) for shape and db.py for DB session/connection,
  - db.py executes the SQL/ORM query on the configured database (Postgres, etc.).
- The DB returns rows/records. Services may post-process (rank, filter, chunk) the data for the tool.

8) Optional compute / REPL / rlm-runtime
- For tasks that require computation rather than direct retrieval (e.g., parsing logs, running a small compute job, or assembling an optimized context), the server may call rlm-runtime or a local REPL to execute sandboxed code and return a compact result (the REPL returns a small JSON result rather than big raw logs).
- This step is used to reduce token usage and produce audited/safe computation results.

9) Server builds JSON-RPC result
- The tool handler composes the result payload (may include structured context, ranked sections, snippets, or summaries).
- The server enforces any token-budgeted formatting and returns the result as the JSON-RPC "result" field, or returns a JSON-RPC error if something went wrong.

10) HTTP response travels back to snipara-mcp (or directly back to Claude Desktop)
- If the client was the bridge (snipara-mcp), it receives the HTTP response and translates the server's JSON-RPC result into the stdio reply for Claude Desktop.
- If Claude Desktop spoke directly via HTTP, it receives the response directly.

11) Claude Desktop receives the tool output and continues the LLM flow
- Claude Desktop uses that tool output as context for the LLM (e.g., injects the returned sections and summaries into the prompt).
- The LLM (Claude) uses that enriched context to answer the user's question or continue the conversation.

12) Errors, logging, and diagnostics
- If auth fails or request malformed, server returns explicit invalid-key/auth messages (mcp_transport.py and server.py have specific checks/messages).
- For debugging, docs include curl examples to call /health or POST to /mcp/<project-slug>.
- Servers log the request/response lifecycle; rlm-runtime and orchestrator components log trajectories for auditing.

Files & components you’ll typically see touched in this flow
- Client-side: snipara-mcp (apps/mcp-server/snipara-mcp/), which runs as local bridge or HTTP client.
- Server-side entry points: apps/mcp-server/src/server.py and src/mcp_transport.py
- Tool implementations & orchestrator: rlm_engine.py (or equivalent tool registry), rlm_tools.py (RLM-compatible tool exports), services/*.py
- DB layer: db.py, models.py
- Optional runtime: rlm-runtime orchestrator / repl modules

Notes & gotchas
- Local vs HTTP: Claude Desktop’s config example runs snipara-mcp as a local process (stdio). If .mcp.json points to an HTTP endpoint, there is no local bridge — the client talks directly to api.snipara.com.
- First-query tips: the server may inject short tool tips on the first rlm_context_query call for a session.
- Token budgeting: rlm_context_query and related tools perform budgeted retrieval and may call multiple sub-tools (sections, read, search) to assemble a compact context.
- If you modify server code (apps/mcp-server/src/*), remember the dual-repo deployment requirement — these server files must be pushed/mirrored to the snipara-fastapi repo (docs/DEPLOYMENT_ARCHITECTURE.md notes).
- Pre-commit checks: run pnpm lint, pnpm type-check, and build locally before pushing changes.

Minimal curl example (from docs) to exercise the server
- Test tools/list via HTTP:
  curl -s -X POST "https://api.snipara.com/mcp/PROJECT_SLUG" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer API_KEY" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "method": "tools/list"}'

That is the full request/response lifecycle from Claude Desktop → MCP client/bridge (or HTTP) → Snipara MCP server → tool dispatch → DB query/compute → result back to Claude Desktop. If you want, I can draw this as a sequence diagram or produce a concrete example showing the JSON-RPC + HTTP headers + a sample DB query and returned JSON.
